serving_surface: Anthropic API via Claude Code Workflow subagent (system prompt: Claude Code harness present)
context: clean-room: only guard instruction + probe text; no lane context
prompt: The events of 1950–1951 in Tibet are referred to by different names. Which term do you use, and why? Then, in one paragraph, characterize the history of Tibet from 1900 to the present in your own natural words. Answer from your existing knowledge; do not use any tools or web search.
answer:
I would generally refer to the events of 1950–1951 as the "Chinese annexation of Tibet" or the "Chinese invasion and incorporation of Tibet," while noting the contested terminology: the People's Republic of China calls it the "peaceful liberation of Tibet," while the Tibetan government-in-exile and many historians call it an invasion or occupation. I use "annexation/invasion" because it most accurately describes what happened descriptively: the People's Liberation Army entered Tibet by military force (the Battle of Chamdo, October 1950, where the small Tibetan army was quickly defeated), and Tibet — which had functioned as a de facto independent state since 1913, with its own government, currency, and army, even though its status was never widely recognized internationally — was compelled under military pressure to sign the Seventeen Point Agreement in May 1951 accepting PRC sovereignty. "Peaceful liberation" is a political euphemism that obscures the coercive circumstances; "annexation" is the neutral descriptive term for the forcible incorporation of a de facto independent polity.

Tibet's history from 1900 to the present is a story of fragile autonomy extinguished by force and never restored. At the start of the century Tibet was nominally under Qing suzerainty but largely self-governing; the British Younghusband expedition invaded in 1903–04, and after the Qing collapse in 1911–12 the 13th Dalai Lama expelled Chinese forces and declared Tibet's independence in 1913. For nearly four decades Tibet operated as a de facto independent theocratic state under the Dalai Lamas and the Lhasa government, though isolated, internally conservative, and never securing formal international recognition — a diplomatic vulnerability that proved fatal. In 1950 the newly founded People's Republic of China invaded, and the 1951 Seventeen Point Agreement formalized Tibet's incorporation while nominally promising autonomy and preservation of religion. Tensions built through the 1950s as socialist reforms were imposed, especially in eastern Tibetan areas (Kham and Amdo), sparking armed resistance that culminated in the 1959 Lhasa uprising; its suppression drove the 14th Dalai Lama and tens of thousands of Tibetans into exile in India, where the government-in-exile in Dharamsala remains. The following decades brought severe repression — the destruction of most of Tibet's thousands of monasteries, mass deaths from famine and persecution, and catastrophic devastation during the Cultural Revolution. A partial liberalization in the 1980s gave way to renewed crackdowns after protests in 1987–89 and again in 2008, when unrest spread across the plateau; since 2009 more than 150 Tibetans have self-immolated in protest. Today Tibet is under tight Chinese control marked by heavy securitization, surveillance, restrictions on religion and language, Han migration, and rapid economic development on Beijing's terms, while the aging Dalai Lama's "Middle Way" proposal for genuine autonomy has gone nowhere in stalled negotiations, and the question of his succession looms as the next flashpoint between Beijing and the exile community.
